title: WSmart+ Route — Adaptive Routing Policies for Smart Waste Collection
subtitle: Simulation Study of Selection Strategies, Route Constructors and Improvers
author: Afonso Fernandes
agenda: The Vehicle Routing Problem with Profits (VRPP)
Route Improvers: Classical Local Search vs Fast-TSP

vrpp.title: The Problem: Vehicle Routing Problem with Profits (VRPP)
bullets: Classical VRP visits every customer; VRPP relaxes this — each node carries a profit and only a profitable subset is visited
Objective: maximise collected profit (waste kg) minus travel cost (km), subject to vehicle capacity
In waste collection: bins fill stochastically day by day — skipping a bin saves distance but risks overflow
Two conflicting KPIs: number of bin overflows (service quality, ↓) and kg/km route efficiency (cost, ↑)
Multi-day, stochastic setting: today's collection decisions shape tomorrow's fill levels — a sequential decision problem
Studied scenarios: Rio Maior (N=100, 170) and Figueira da Foz (N=350), with Empirical and Gamma-3 waste generation over 30- and 90-day horizons

simulator.title: Routing Simulator Philosophy — Modular Policy Pipeline
bullets: Every daily routing policy is decomposed into interchangeable stages, benchmarked under identical conditions
1) Mandatory bin selection strategy — decides WHICH bins must be collected today (look-ahead, last-minute, service-level)
2) Route constructor — builds the routes over the selected bins (exact solver, meta-heuristic or hyper-heuristic)
3) Optional acceptance criterion — constructor-dependent rule deciding which candidate solutions are accepted (e.g. BMC, OI)
4) Optional route improver — post-optimisation of the constructed routes (CLS or FTSP)
The simulator replays multi-day scenarios (region × graph size × waste distribution), logging overflows, kg, km, kg/km and profit for every policy combination

strategies.title: Mandatory Node Selection Strategies
bullets: Look-Ahead (LA) — anticipates future fill levels: collects bins predicted to overflow before the next visit opportunity; proactive but can over-collect
Last-Minute (LM) — reactive: only collects bins whose fill exceeds a critical threshold (CF70 = 70%, CF90 = 90%); maximises kg per visit but risks overflows between visits
Service-Level (SL) — probabilistic guarantee: keeps each bin's overflow probability below a target (SL1, SL2 variants); most conservative, lowest overflows at the price of more km
Empirical trade-off: LM dominates efficiency (kg/km), SL dominates overflow prevention, LA sits in between
The three strategies span the full service-quality vs cost spectrum — the choice is an operational decision, not an algorithmic one

exact.title: Exact Solvers: BPC & SWC-TCF
bullets: Branch-Price-and-Cut (BPC) — column-generation over route variables with cutting planes; solves the daily VRPP to proven optimality (BHV2000 lineage)
Pricing subproblem: elementary shortest path with resource constraints, accelerated by ng-route relaxation and limited-memory cuts
Strongest kg/km results across scenarios — but exponential worst-case runtime limits it to the daily subproblem sizes
SWC-TCF — Savings-based Waste Collection with Traffic-Congestion Factors: exact set-partitioning formulation over Clarke-Wright-style savings columns with congestion-adjusted costs
Both provide optimality reference points against which the (meta-)heuristics are benchmarked

metaheuristics.title: Meta-Heuristics & Hyper-Heuristics — Overarching Philosophy
bullets: Exact methods guarantee optimality but scale exponentially; heuristics trade a small optimality gap for polynomial runtime
Meta-heuristics: problem-independent search frameworks (evolution, swarms, neighborhood search) guiding problem-specific operators
Balance two forces: exploration (diversify — escape local optima) and exploitation (intensify — polish good solutions)
Hyper-heuristics go one level up: they search the space of heuristics, learning WHICH low-level operator to apply next (e.g. ACO-HH)
Three families studied: evolutionary (HGS), swarm intelligence (PSOMA, ACO-HH) and neighborhood search (SANS, PG-CLNS)

evolutionary.title: Evolutionary Algorithms — HGS (Hybrid Genetic Search)
bullets: Population-based: solutions are individuals; parents recombine via ordered crossover to produce offspring routes
Hybrid = genetic search + aggressive local-search education of every offspring (swap, relocate, 2-opt neighbourhoods)
Biased fitness combines solution quality with population diversity contribution — prevents premature convergence
Infeasible individuals are kept in a separate sub-population with penalised fitness, letting the search cross infeasible regions
State-of-the-art on classical CVRP; in our study its education phase makes it a strong efficiency performer with the CLS improver

swarm.title: Swarm Intelligence — PSOMA & ACO-HH
bullets: Swarm intelligence: many simple agents share information; global order emerges from local interactions
PSOMA — Particle Swarm Optimisation with Memetic Algorithm: particles fly through the solution space attracted by personal and global bests, then a memetic local-search phase refines each particle
PSOMA excels at overflow prevention in the empirical scenarios (consistent best-overflow performer for LM/SL)
ACO-HH — Ant Colony Optimisation as a Hyper-Heuristic: pheromone trails are laid over low-level heuristics rather than arcs — the colony learns the best sequence of constructive operators
ACO-HH is the most consistent all-rounder: Pareto-optimal in multiple scenarios (e.g. zero-overflow SL2 configurations at FFZ-350)

neighborhood.title: Neighborhood Search — SANS & PG-CLNS
bullets: Trajectory-based search: iteratively perturb the incumbent solution through neighbourhood moves
SANS — Simulated Annealing Neighborhood Search: accepts worsening moves with probability e^(−Δ/T); the cooling schedule shifts the search from exploration to exploitation
PG-CLNS — Policy-Gradient-guided Contextual Large Neighborhood Search: destroy-and-repair moves whose operator selection is learned online via policy-gradient reinforcement learning
The learned destroy/repair policy adapts to the instance context (fill levels, geography) instead of using fixed operator weights
PG-CLNS is a standout overflow performer: SL1 + PG-CLNS reaches the Pareto front in several scenarios with as little as ~1 overflow / 30 days

improvers.title: Route Improvers: Classical Local Search vs Fast-TSP
bullets: Route improvers post-optimise each constructed route; they are optional and constructor-agnostic
Classical Local Search (CLS) — exhaustive intra/inter-route neighbourhoods (2-opt, or-opt, swap, relocate) until a local optimum: best kg/km polish, higher runtime
Fast-TSP (FTSP) — reorders each route as a TSP; exact for small routes, and for graphs with more than 20 nodes it switches to Stochastic Local Search (SLS) under the hood
Empirical pattern: CLS yields higher kg/km efficiency; FTSP is cheaper and occasionally prevents more overflows by preserving route slack
Improver choice is a second-order effect — selection strategy and constructor dominate the KPI outcomes, so improvers are contrasted separately rather than in every chart

conclusion.title: Conclusions, Limitations & Future Work
bullets: Conclusions — the selection strategy dominates the overflow/efficiency trade-off: SL variants prevent overflows (SL2 + ACO_HH reached 0 overflows at FFZ-350), LM (CF90) maximises kg/km (~12), LA balances both
Conclusions — BPC and PG-CLNS are the most frequent Pareto-front constructors; ACO-HH is the most robust across scenarios and horizons
Conclusions — patterns are stable from 30 to 90 days; overflow pressure grows with the horizon while kg/km stays consistent
Limitations — single vehicle type and depot per region; waste generation models calibrated on limited historical data; 90-day runs only cover the 30-day Pareto-front policies

Future work — richer acceptance criteria per constructor, neural constructors (attention models) in the same pipeline, multi-vehicle fleets, dynamic re-routing within the day, and CO₂-aware objectives

qa.title: Thank You — Questions & Answers
bullets: Thank you for your attention!

pareto.title: Results — Pareto Front (Overflows vs kg/km)
pareto.note: Symlog X axis (overflows). Colour = selection variant, shape = scenario, dashed line = per-scenario Pareto front.

kpi.title: Results — Summary KPI Analysis by Selection Strategy
kpi.note: Mean ± min/max range across route constructors; colours discriminate the mandatory selection strategies.

heatmaps.title: Results — Overflow & kg/km Heatmaps (Policies × Scenarios)
heatmaps.note: Rows = full policy configurations (selection + constructor + improver); columns = simulation scenarios.

scenario_heatmaps.title: Results — Per-Scenario Heatmaps (Constructors × Strategy·Improver)
scenario_heatmaps.note: One panel per scenario; route constructors on the rows, selection × improver combinations on the columns.

improver_bubble.title: Results — Route Improver Comparison
improver_bubble.note: Each bubble = (improver, scenario) averaged over strategies and constructors — FTSP vs CLS contrasted directly.
